text: We present a video on the uses of ICG fluorescence imaging technology in complex inflammatory bowel disease surgery.
We use this technology with patients who have a high-risk anastomosis, meaning they are on steroids, biologics, or have multiple segmental resections.
And the success of that anastomosis may mean the difference between a permanent ileostomy or not.
The first patient was a thirty-one-year-old male with fulminant ulcerative colitis who progressed through medical management.
The surgeon performs ICG fluorescence imaging on the rectal stump to assess its viability before implanting it into the subcutaneous tissues.
The surgeon attaches the bowel to the fascia, a technique aimed at preventing pelvic sepsis by securing the bowel in place.
However, we must be certain we're not causing ischemia in a diseased bowel that we're leaving in situ.
Our second case is a young male with complex fistulizing Crohn's disease.
He progressed through medical management and was taken to the operating room for a complex resection.
The surgical field reveals multiple enterocolic and entero-entero fistulas, along with a perforation that is visibly sealed by the omentum.
The surgeon divides the thickened Crohn's mesentery using interlocking number one chromic sutures, carefully separating the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon injects indocyanine green (ICG) to assess the viability of the end-to-side stapled ileocolic anastomosis, ensuring adequate blood flow and tissue perfusion.
The patient was discharged uneventfully after we corrected his eye output ileostomy with medical therapy.
Our third patient had complex fistulizing ileocolic disease and progressed through medical therapy.
He was taken to the operating room for a complex resection.
The surgeon injects indocyanine green (ICG) dye to assess the viability of the ileocolic anastomosis, observing the fluorescence to ensure proper blood flow and tissue perfusion.
The surgeon creates an omental pedicle graft, manipulating the omentum to form a graft for potential reinforcement or coverage of the ileocolic anastomosis.
He was discharged on post-operative day ten after we medically managed his high output ileostomy.
Our fourth patient is a patient who underwent a redo J-pouch procedure after we found mechanical evidence for J-pouch dysfunction rather than her initial diagnosis of Crohn's of the pouch.
The imaging reveals a large, dilated J-pouch with visible fecalization within the pouch structure.
Her outlet obstruction caused pouch dilation and a pseudo twist.
The surgeon untwists the dilated pouch, correcting the pseudo twist observed in the previous imaging.
We were able to perform pouch augmentation and not have to create a new pouch.
The surgeon injects indocyanine green (ICG) into the pouch to assess its viability, observing the fluorescence to evaluate blood flow and tissue health.
The surgeon excises the scar at the distal end of the pouch and performs a hand-sewn anastomosis, visually connecting the tissue edges.
The patient was discharged on post-operative day twenty after a prolonged ileus.
There is currently no suspicion for Crohn's of her pouch.
Our last case is a twenty-year-old male with a history of obesity who also had ulcerative colitis and needed a J-pouch.
Because of his obesity, his pouch reach to his pelvis was an issue.
We performed pouch lengthening procedures but wanted to assess the viability of the pouch after performing these pouch lengthening procedures.
There were no problems with blood supply and the patient did well post-operatively.

In conclusion, although recent research has been specifically focused on ICG with colorectal anastomosis, we have demonstrated additional applications of ICG technology specifically for the IBD population.
